examples.title: General Tree Search Algorithm
examples.description: Template for all tree-based search algorithms
examples.code:
FUNCTION Tree-Search(problem, fringe) RETURN solution or failure
 fringe ← INSERT(MAKE-NODE(problem.INITIAL-STATE), fringe)
 WHILE fringe is not empty DO
 node ← REMOVE-FRINGE(fringe)
 IF problem.GOAL-TEST(node.STATE) THEN
 RETURN SOLUTION(node)
 fringe ← INSERT_ALL(EXPAND(node, problem), fringe)
 END WHILE
 RETURN failure

FUNCTION EXPAND(node, problem) RETURN set of nodes
 successors ← empty set
 FOR EACH action IN problem.ACTIONS(node.STATE) DO
 state ← problem.RESULT(node.STATE, action)
 child ← NODE(state, parent=node, action=action, path-cost=node.PATH-COST + problem.ACTION-COST)
 successors ← INSERT(child, successors)
 RETURN successors
examples.explanation: Basic framework for uninformed search. The fringe (frontier) data structure determines algorithm type: queue for BFS, stack for DFS, priority queue for uniform-cost/A*.

examples.title: A* Search Algorithm
examples.description: Optimal informed search using heuristic evaluation
examples.code:
FUNCTION A-STAR-SEARCH(problem) RETURN solution or failure
 node ← MAKE-NODE(problem.INITIAL-STATE)
 g ← 0 // Path cost from start
 f ← g + problem.HEURISTIC(node.STATE)
 frontier ← PRIORITY_QUEUE ordered by f
 frontier.INSERT(node, f)
 explored ← empty set
 
 WHILE frontier is not empty DO
 node ← frontier.POP()
 
 IF problem.GOAL-TEST(node.STATE) THEN
 RETURN SOLUTION(node)
 
 explored.ADD(node.STATE)
 
 FOR EACH child IN EXPAND(node, problem) DO
 child.g ← node.g + problem.ACTION-COST
 child.f ← child.g + problem.HEURISTIC(child.STATE)
 
 // Skip if already explored or worse path in frontier
 IF child.STATE NOT IN explored AND NOT IN frontier THEN
 frontier.INSERT(child, child.f)
 ELSE IF child.STATE IN frontier WITH HIGHER f THEN
 frontier.REPLACE(old-node, child, child.f)
 END WHILE
 
 RETURN failure
examples.explanation: A* combines actual path cost g(n) with heuristic estimate h(n) to find optimal solution. Uses priority queue ordered by f(n) = g(n) + h(n). Complete and optimal if h is admissible.

examples.title: Hill Climbing Algorithm
examples.code:
FUNCTION HILL-CLIMBING(problem) RETURN state (possibly goal)
 current ← MAKE-NODE(problem.INITIAL-STATE)
 
 LOOP
 neighbor ← null
 best-value ← EVALUATE(current)
 
 FOR EACH successor IN EXPAND(current, problem) DO
 value ← EVALUATE(successor)
 IF value > best-value THEN
 neighbor ← successor
 best-value ← value
 
 IF best-value <= EVALUATE(current) THEN
 RETURN current.STATE // No better neighbor, return current
 
 current ← neighbor // Move to better neighbor
 END LOOP

// Variant with random restarts
FUNCTION HILL-CLIMBING-RESTART(problem, limit) RETURN solution
 FOR i = 1 TO limit DO
 current ← RANDOM-INITIAL-STATE()
 result ← HILL-CLIMBING(current)
 IF problem.GOAL-TEST(result) THEN
 RETURN result
 RETURN failure
examples.explanation: Local search that examines neighbors and moves to better state. Simple but gets stuck in local maxima. Random restarts improve chances of finding global optimum.

examples.title: Iterative Deepening A* (IDA*)
examples.description: Memory-efficient A* using depth-first search with f-cost limit
examples.code:
FUNCTION IDA-STAR(problem) RETURN solution or failure
 threshold ← problem.HEURISTIC(problem.INITIAL-STATE)
 
 LOOP
 result ← RECURSIVE-DFS(problem.INITIAL-STATE, 0, threshold)
 
 IF result = FOUND THEN RETURN solution
 IF result = ∞ THEN RETURN failure // No solution exists
 
 threshold ← result // Next threshold is minimum f-value exceeded
 END LOOP

FUNCTION RECURSIVE-DFS(node, g, threshold) RETURN found or new-threshold
 f ← g + problem.HEURISTIC(node.STATE)
 
 IF f > threshold THEN RETURN f // Prune this branch
 
 IF problem.GOAL-TEST(node.STATE) THEN RETURN FOUND
 
 min ← ∞
 FOR EACH successor IN EXPAND(node, problem) DO
 result ← RECURSIVE-DFS(successor, g + cost, threshold)
 IF result = FOUND THEN RETURN FOUND
 IF result < min THEN min ← result
 
 RETURN min
examples.explanation: IDA* eliminates A* memory requirements by using iterative depth-first with f-cost thresholds. Each iteration searches with f ≤ threshold, then increases threshold to minimum f that exceeded previous limit.
